title: Hanging wallpaper: pasting-table method
subtitle: Plumb first, then work outward from a true line
excerpt: How to hang standard paste-the-paper wallpaper in a room: establishing a plumb starting line, cutting lengths, pasting and booking the paper, hanging and trimming. Covers pattern matching, internal and external corners, and reading the join in the right light.
sourceNotes: Standard UK hanging technique from Collins Complete DIY Manual and the City & Guilds Painting and Decorating Level 2 syllabus. Pattern-repeat and booking conventions unchanged from Victorian trade practice described in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

recipeTools.notes: For pressing paper into the skirting and ceiling angle before trimming.
For rubbing down any raised areas on the wall before papering.

content.text: Hanging wallpaper is slower than painting but the process is methodical. The critical decisions are at the beginning: which wall to start on, where to place the first length, and whether the pattern repeat allows a centred or stepped start. Every length after the first is governed by those decisions.
Mix the paste according to the weight of the paper: heavier papers need a thicker mix. Standard paste comes as a powder.
A dedicated wallpaper pasting table keeps the length flat while you paste. A full-length table prevents the paper curling off the ends.
For applying paste to the paper. A dedicated paste brush; do not use a painting brush.
For smoothing the hung length flat against the wall and into the angles.
For pressing the joins flat once the paper has been hung. Do not use on embossed papers.
For establishing the first plumb line. Never rely on the corner of a room; it is almost never truly vertical.
For trimming at the ceiling line and skirting. A blunt blade drags the paper.

Fill any cracks and sand them back. Strip old wallpaper and wash off any paste residue. Size the walls with diluted PVA or a proprietary size to equalize suction and give the paste a workable open time. Allow to dry.

Measure one roll width (typically 530 mm for UK standard rolls) from the first corner you plan to paper from, and draw a vertical pencil line using the spirit level. This is your plumb datum. Every first length goes alongside it; every subsequent length butts from the previous one.

Measure the height of the wall from ceiling to skirting. Add 100 mm for trimming allowance. For a pattern repeat, add one full repeat length per cut to ensure you can match the pattern at the top. Label each length on the reverse with its hanging order and which way up it goes.

Lay the first length face-down on the pasting table. Apply paste from the centre outward to the far edge, then reposition the paper so the other edge overhangs the table and paste that edge. Cover the length fully; dry edges or bubbles under the surface mean missed paste. Check that the ends are fully pasted.

Fold the pasted side loosely to itself in thirds (pasted side inward) and leave it on the table for the soak time specified on the roll label, typically 5 to 10 minutes. This is booking. The soak allows the paper to expand evenly before hanging; hanging before the soak time causes bubbles that shrink back as the paste dries.

Unfold the top half of the booked length. Position it against the plumb line with 50 mm overlapping the ceiling. Smooth the top section flat against the wall with the papering brush, working from the centre outward to push out air. Unfold the lower half, align it with the plumb line, and smooth down. Use the brush to push the paper firmly into the ceiling and skirting angles.

Press the overlap into the ceiling angle with the back edge of the scraper blade to create a crease. Pull the paper gently away from the wall and cut along the crease with a sharp knife. Repeat at the skirting. Smooth the trimmed edges back into place.

Paste, soak, and hang each subsequent length butted tightly against the previous one so the edges are flush. Run the seam roller lightly down the join. Do not use the seam roller on embossed papers; it crushes the pattern. Continue around the room.

Measure the gap between the last length and the corner at the top, middle, and bottom of the wall. Cut the final length to the widest of those three measurements, plus 10 mm wrap. Paste and hang this piece, brushing the 10 mm wrap around the corner. Then establish a new plumb line on the next wall and start the fresh wall from it. This absorbs any out-of-plumb corner.
Wrap the paper at least 20 mm around the corner. Start the next wall with a new plumb line as with an internal corner.
Bubbles that do not shrink back.
Either the paper was not soaked long enough, or the paste was too thin and some areas dried before the paper expanded. Peel the bubble area back gently while still wet and re-paste; smooth down.

This usually means one length was hung slightly out of plumb or the soak time was inconsistent, causing one length to expand more than the other. Establish plumb on every third or fourth length as a check, not just the first.
Paste on the face of the paper.
Keep a clean damp sponge on the pasting table. Wipe the face of each length before hanging and clean the table before cutting the next length.
